name: Business Topology — Rodrigo's Structural Self-Description (from recorded conversation)
subject: A compressed, 33-point extraction of Rodrigo's own structural self-description of Comeketo — the operating areas, tools, rhythms, relationship layers, and strategic posture — captured from a recorded founder conversation and then aggregated into a single business picture. This is not raw data. It is the founder's own framing of how the business works, which carries different weight than any spreadsheet or deck: it's how the operation sees itself. Rodbot should treat it as a high-trust reference for judgment calls about scope, priorities, and what 'normal operations' look like. Where this document confirms the existing bedrock it reinforces the pattern; where it adds new fact it fills gaps; where it surfaces something the other files don't mention it opens a new thread to pull.

source_context.origin: Earlier conversation Jake had with an AI assistant, put on record, then compressed and aggregated into a structured business picture (the document supplied by Jake on 2026-04-22).
source_context.speaker: Rodrigo (implied — founder framing in first person throughout the extraction)
source_context.reliability_caveat: This is a transcript-derived compression, not an audited document. Treat facts as founder-self-report. The compression editor (an AI) explicitly flagged points that 'weren't fully captured in the recording but Jake confirmed separately' — specifically point 31 (social media). Respect those markers.
source_context.canonical_weight: HIGH — this is the founder's framing of his own business. When in doubt between what a sheet says and what Rodrigo said, weight the founder's stated intent unless the sheet is a system of record.

main_systems: Google Sheets (many, some shared / some private / some management-only / plus a sheet-registry sheet)
Mixed Google / Outlook environment (domain and some email on Microsoft, most ops on Google)
main_business_characteristics: Visual operational tracking (color / layout as comprehension aid)

structural_observations.observation: Business is not chaotic. It already runs through a combination of: sales pipeline management, calendar-based event execution, labor and staffing coordination, venue and referral relationship management, prep and shopping coordination, weekly financial visibility, role-based task management, shared Google-based operating surfaces. The company already has information structure, workflow structure, reporting structure, and ownership structure. Well-instrumented manually.

A major part of the business is organized around incoming leads, deal flow, and weekly close visibility. Tracked: deals closed per week, where leads came from, time-to-close, locked-in leads, closed deals, source attribution, lead ownership, tasting-linked sales activity. Already has a functioning sales intelligence layer, even if manual.

Source tracking matters a lot — Rodrigo is not just interested in whether leads come in, but which sources produce them, volume per source, what's converting, which sources are strongest. The business thinks in terms of acquisition channels, referral quality, source effectiveness.
Close CRM is one of the core operating systems: lead tracking, close tracking, source tracking, ownership visibility, sales pipeline. Not peripheral — a sales authority in the business.

Rodrigo keeps a lead tracker that logs incoming leads AND predicts cash flow. It estimates how much money should be expected, what should be coming in during a given week, and the relationship between current pipeline and future cash. Sales-state information is LINKED to operational finance — not separate systems.

Labor cost is actively monitored at a weekly level: what the week is costing, who is being used, how much labor is being consumed across activity. Labor is not treated as background overhead — staffing decisions are tied to real operational planning.

Weekly schedule reflects work allocation and team distribution. Team size changes seasonally — around 4-5 during the conversation period, scales up during busier periods. Can have multiple jobs in multiple places simultaneously. Real staffing, allocation, and timing problems — not a simple one-location operation.

Google Calendar is central: jobs are pulled from the calendar as the basis for weekly work planning. Calendar is the temporal backbone of the company — knowing what jobs are coming, staging upcoming execution, seeing what the team has to do.

Every job is a bundle of operational requirements, not just 'an event.' Tracked per job: party size, client name, grilling-on-site yes/no, food and bar requirements, food address / location context, event timeline, setup time, travel time, who is doing the job, banquet event order link, cocktail/dinner/timeline structure. Labor, prep, travel, timing, and difficulty are all derived from these details.

Rodrigo values fast operational comprehension. Uses color and visual layout so the eye can quickly understand the full story of a job. Complexity is interpreted visually, not just numerically. Practical human-interface logic: make complexity legible quickly.

Not all events are equal. Examples of complexity drivers: water service, grill-on-site, special handling, prep/logistics burden. The business already thinks in terms of simple event / more demanding event / higher-logistics event / more staff-intensive event. There is an implicit internal complexity model.
When people spend money out of pocket on jobs, there is a reimbursement tracking sheet. Expense handling is distributed, not fully centralized. Admin/finance side has access to it.

Practical weekly money awareness: one sheet tracks what should be coming OUT of the account, another tracks what should be coming IN. Rodrigo looks at weekly ranges and compares expected withdrawals to actual timing. Not abstract accounting — week-by-week operational cash visibility.

A shopping list shared with the chef doubles as a prep list: what food needs to be purchased, quantities, what needs to be prepared that week. Procurement and prep are actively coordinated and tied to the weekly event schedule.

Managers and other people have their own daily/weekly sheets for organization. Person-specific work surfaces, role-based task management, distributed responsibility. Not everything funnels through one master dashboard.

Venues are the company's #1 referral source. Comeketo maintains a venue list, partner venues, repeated venues, intentional relationship building, reps assigned to specific venues, monthly/regular check-ins. Goal: be valuable to venues, easy to work with, top of mind, a preferred partner. Venue relations are a strategic growth mechanism.
Event planners are grouped with venues as relationship surfaces to cultivate. Referral/relationship system extends beyond places to include the people who influence bookings.

Venues are assigned to sales reps so they can curate the relationship. Relationships have owners, follow-up is structured, partner development is delegated. Not casual networking — becoming an internal process.

Preferred-vendor setup is used to quickly recommend options based on location. Trusted partner network, geographically contextual, recommendations are part of the sales / service conversation. Internal knowledge of trusted external entities is a practical asset.

Tastings happen TWICE A WEEK, with people coming in physically to a hall. Include presentation, direct interaction, opportunity to close on the spot, tracking of person / lead owner / outcome. Strong conversion rate once people come in. A structured pipeline stage, not a casual extra.
Commission reports for the sales team exist. Sales outcomes tie into compensation, explicit rep accountability, commissions tracked. Sales reporting matters beyond just closing work.

Business runs on a large number of Google Sheets — shared, management-only, personal, operational, reference. There's even a sheet to manage the sheets. Deeply spreadsheet-native. Messy in one sense, valuable in another — the business has already encoded its logic into a large manual record system.

Despite the Outlook/Microsoft split on email/domain, most business operations live in Google: Sheets, Calendar, shared business information. Google is one of the major operational homes of the business.

Part of the team is on Outlook / GoDaddy / Microsoft while another part is on Google. Mixed environment, not a clean single-stack. Suggests duplication, fragmentation, possible communication friction, a need for eventual consolidation.
Two admin people, four salespeople, one remote sales manager, one marketing component, operations, remote team members. Already segmented into functional roles — not one owner doing everything.

Rodrigo's thinking is already organizationally layered: departments, positions, people, responsibilities, how information flows between people. Thinks in terms of who owns what, how groups relate, how work is distributed, where coordination is needed.
Management is active and operational, not symbolic. Managers have structured work surfaces, recurring duties, organized activity tracking.
The business is understood week by week: weekly schedule, weekly labor, weekly expected cash, weekly prep, weekly jobs, weekly comparison windows. Strong weekly cadence is a major structural fact.

Company runs on repeated cycles: lead intake → follow-up → tasting → close → event scheduling → staffing → shopping/prep → execution → reimbursement/expenses → weekly cash awareness → venue relationship maintenance. Highly cycle-driven rather than ad hoc.

Rodrigo is already trying to create order out of complexity — grouping information visually, summarizing jobs side by side, tracking partners, assigning venues to reps, building shopping lists, tracking labor, forecasting cash, maintaining lead trackers, organizing people-specific sheets. Operational intelligence instincts, building management structure manually.

Social media matters more than the transcript fully captured — Rodrigo was especially excited about it per Jake's supplemental clarification. Channels mentioned: YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, Twitter. Business has a visibility/growth/social dimension, not just operations.
structural_observations.caveat: Not fully represented in the transcript itself — included because Jake flagged it separately as something Rodrigo was excited about.

structural_observations.observation: Business is a strong candidate for templating — the combination of sales + pipeline + events + staffing + labor + prep + finance + venue/planner relationships + referral-based growth + social + spreadsheet-native operations could become a reference model for similar businesses.

The company is not broken — it is manually overextended. Rodrigo has built a lot of systems manually and reached the limit of what can be managed by hand. State: operationally rich, manually tracked, fragmented across tools, dependent on human vigilance, already structured enough to support something much more powerful.

confirms_existing_bedrock.bedrock_anchor: knowledge/venue_partners.json — already tracks ~20+ venues with events-completed counts, rep ownership, and tier assignments. This document reinforces the strategic primacy.
knowledge/venue_partners.json — 'owner' fields on each venue record (e.g., 'Mechanics Hall — owner: Andre'). Now confirmed as deliberate process, not accidental tagging.
CLAUDE.md project instructions explicitly name Close as 'ALLOWED and primary' and 'Comeketo's admin lives in Close.' The founder conversation independently confirms.

confirms_existing_bedrock.point: Lead source attribution as a core concern (section 3)
confirms_existing_bedrock.bedrock_anchor: knowledge/leads_source_dec_2025.json — captures 58 Dec 2025 leads with channel attribution across 8+ sources. Now confirmed as a deliberate priority, not just a reporting artifact.

knowledge/kitchen_schedule_feb16_2026.json (weekly kitchen schedule), knowledge/event_labor_projection_weekly.json (weekly labor projection), knowledge/cash_flow_revenue_projection.json (weekly buckets across 100 weeks). The weekly rhythm shows up everywhere in the data and is now confirmed as the founder's explicit mental model.
knowledge/infrastructure_inventory.json documents ~20 internal sheets AND the meta-'sheet for the sheets'. Confirms the founder's framing exactly.

confirms_existing_bedrock.point: Tasting as a structured pipeline stage (section 20)
confirms_existing_bedrock.bedrock_anchor: knowledge/tasting_conversion_tracking.json + the Welcome-Rodrigo deck's tasting-day bonus reveal + the sales playbook's 'tasting is the mechanism' framing. The twice-a-week cadence is new specificity.
knowledge/sales_team_scorecard_oct_2025.json — already captures per-rep performance multipliers. Commission reports are a natural adjacent artifact, now confirmed to exist.

people/ records (Andre, Eduarda, Ren, Toni = 4 salespeople — matches section 25 exactly), plus the newly-added Camila/Anne/Domenic management records. The 'two admin people' and 'one remote sales manager' are now explicit to map.

knowledge/event_labor_projection_weekly.json + knowledge/kitchen_schedule_feb16_2026.json — both capture per-event detail including guest count, location, grill-on-site, labor allotment. The founder conversation articulates WHY those fields exist.

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Tastings happen TWICE A WEEK at a hall.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: No other indexed file states the cadence explicitly. This is important for capacity planning — twice-a-week means ~104 tasting slots per year, which constrains how many pipeline conversions are physically possible. Also cross-references the owned-venue project (the 'hall' here may be the same 'THIS VERY VENUE' from slide 48).

new_facts_surfaced.fact: The team is approximately 4-5 people during normal periods, scaling up during busy periods.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: The sales team alone is 4 people. If total team is 4-5 normally, that's essentially sales + admin with seasonal staff scaling. Reconcile with staff_labor_roster.json (which lists 17 people — likely total roster including seasonal/event staff, not a headcount at any given moment).

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Team composition detail: two admin people, four salespeople, one remote sales manager, one marketing component, operations, remote team members.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: Explicit organizational decomposition. Maps four salespeople (Andre, Eduarda, Ren, Toni — confirmed). Flags an unresolved role: 'one remote sales manager' — this is a person we don't have a record for. Marketing component suggests someone like Eufy/Rena/Rhonna owns the marketing function. 'Two admin people' likely maps to Cathlyn + one other.

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Lead tracker is ALSO a cash-flow forecasting instrument.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: Previously the lead tracker and the cash-flow sheet were indexed as separate artifacts. The founder's framing treats them as operationally linked — pipeline value flows into cash expectation. Any agent that surfaces a stale lead tracker is implicitly surfacing a stale cash forecast too.

The Partnership Program framework mentions preferred vendors, but this document specifies that recommendations are geographically contextual — Rodrigo uses location to pick which partner to recommend. That's a practical logic Rodbot can use when surfacing partner suggestions to a couple based on their venue.

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Reimbursement tracking exists as a distinct sheet.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: Not indexed yet. Flag as candidate raw-data request. Matters for the Rodbot scope if anyone asks about reimbursements or out-of-pocket expenses.

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Commission reports exist as a distinct artifact.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: Not indexed yet. Flag as candidate raw-data request. Matters for any rep-performance conversation — the scorecard KPIs are one view, the commission report is another.

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Mixed Outlook/GoDaddy + Google environment — some of the team is on Outlook, most ops on Google.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: Reconciles the infrastructure inventory's Microsoft 365 seat listing with the Google Sheets/Calendar reliance. Confirms that M365 seats are for email/domain while Google is the work-surface. Has implications for where Rodbot routes outbound (Gmail vs Outlook) and which system is canonical for calendar lookups (Google).

new_facts_surfaced.fact: Social media is a growth priority: YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, Twitter.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: The indexed material shows IG outreach and FB groups activity, but no YouTube/TikTok/LinkedIn/Twitter presence is documented. Gap: the social presence across those five channels is not yet in bedrock. Could be a major raw-data pull once the channels exist (handles, content cadence, performance metrics).

new_facts_surfaced.fact: The business is manually overextended — structure exists, but human vigilance is a failure mode.
new_facts_surfaced.why_matters: This is the founder's own diagnosis. It sets the bar for what Rodbot should DO: reduce the cognitive load on humans, make visible what was buried in sheets, notice drift before a human would. It also sets what Rodbot should NOT do: add a new surface that requires more vigilance.

gaps_and_flags.gap: The 'remote sales manager' — named as a distinct role but no person record exists.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Ask Rodrigo who this is. Possibly one of the existing salespeople functioning as a lead, or a separate person not yet in bedrock. Create a people/ record once identified.

gaps_and_flags.gap: The 'two admin people' — one is likely Cathlyn; the second is unnamed.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Ask Rodrigo for the second admin name. Candidates from the infrastructure inventory's sheet-registry signals: Rhonna, Eufy, Rena (the non-sales management-layer cluster).

gaps_and_flags.gap: The 'marketing component' — not a named person yet.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Likely one of Eufy / Rena / Rhonna based on their IG Insight + FB Groups sheet access. Confirm with Rodrigo.

gaps_and_flags.gap: Commission report sheet — not yet indexed.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Candidate P1 raw-data request. Rodrigo or Cathlyn likely owns.

gaps_and_flags.gap: Social presence (YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn, Twitter) — no channel handles, content inventory, or performance data in bedrock.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: When Rodrigo prioritizes social as a growth area, pull channel-by-channel account inventories + performance snapshots. Flag as a distinct future indexing arc.

gaps_and_flags.gap: The 'hall' where tastings happen twice a week — is this the owned venue referenced in the Welcome deck's slide 48?
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Cross-reference: if tastings happen at 'the hall' and the Welcome deck says 'THIS VERY VENUE — Officially Opens this Spring,' the tasting hall IS probably the owned venue. Unlock for projects/comeketo_owned_venue.json.

gaps_and_flags.gap: Total team size ambiguity — 4-5 normal per founder vs 17 in staff_labor_roster.json.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Reconcile: the 17 is likely the full roster including seasonal event staff and kitchen crew, while '4-5' is the core office/ops + sales team on any given day. Worth a one-line reconciliation note in the roster file.

gaps_and_flags.gap: Event complexity model is implicit but not codified.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Consider structuring an event_complexity_tiers knowledge card later — simple / standard / high-logistics / staff-intensive — with criteria like grill-on-site, water service, travel distance, party size. Rodbot could score events on the fly for staffing forecasts.

gaps_and_flags.gap: Calendar is described as 'core source of operational truth' but no calendar data is in bedrock.
gaps_and_flags.next_action: Google Calendar connector integration — when wired, Rodbot can see jobs and staffing against the weekly rhythm the founder described.

what_this_shifts_in_rodbot_posture: Rodbot should assume the founder's mental model IS the business's mental model. Weekly rhythm, cycle thinking, venue primacy, source attribution discipline — these are not incidental, they're the operating system.

Rodbot should actively reduce the cognitive load on Rodrigo and the management layer. The founder diagnosed the business as 'manually overextended.' Every Rodbot action should either (a) surface what was buried, or (b) automate a piece of vigilance that's currently human-consumed. Never the opposite.
Rodbot should default to weekly framing when talking about operations internally. 'This week' / 'next week' / 'the last six weeks' — not daily, not quarterly, unless the context demands.

Rodbot should respect the role-based task-sheet pattern. When surfacing work for a specific person, match their existing surface rather than creating a new one. Respect the visual-readability principle — complexity-legible-quickly beats detailed-but-dense.

Rodbot should treat the venue-planner-vendor partnership layer as the company's #1 growth mechanism. Any B2B outbound should ladder into venue / planner / preferred-vendor cultivation. The partnership program framework becomes the practical engine, not a nice-to-have.
how_this_sharpens_the_character_layer: character.md's 'no theater, warmth in the noticing' posture is now stronger — the founder's style IS observation-first (he built visual shorthand, color-coded sheets, side-by-side job views all for the purpose of fast comprehension). Rodbot's noticing mirrors that instinct.

character.md's 'curious, not transactional' opener (B2C signature move #7) aligns with Rodrigo's source-attribution discipline. When a lead comes in, asking 'what made you reach out to us specifically?' isn't just a sales move — it's also a data-capture move that feeds the lead-source tracker.

traits.md's team-posture toward Camila (partnerships) and Anne (events / forecasting) are now more anchored — the founder's framing confirms partnerships are a dedicated function and the weekly forecast is an operational instrument, not a finance artifact.
what_it_rules_out_for_rodbot: Do not propose tools or workflows that ADD manual vigilance. Founder diagnosis is clear — the business is already overextended.
Do not treat venues as transactional one-off bookings. They are the #1 referral source and relationship ownership is assigned by rep — any Rodbot touch on a venue threads through that rep.

Do not treat tasting as a soft next-step for a warm lead. The founder confirmed tastings are a STRUCTURED PIPELINE STAGE with strong conversion. A tasting booking is a significant event, not a friendly coffee.

notes: This document came from Jake as the output of a prior compression/aggregation pass — so it already reflects Jake's own judgment about what mattered enough to preserve. Treat the 33-section scaffold as load-bearing: it was curated.

Where the compression flagged a caveat (point 31 on social, point 32 on templatability), the caveat is preserved. Rodbot should respect those markers and not overstate those points in downstream reasoning.
This file intentionally does not try to resolve the social-media growth angle — it flags it as a distinct future arc. Bedrock should receive a separate pass whenever Rodrigo prioritizes that lane.
The 'manually overextended' framing (point 33) is the operational thesis of this entire document. If Rodbot only remembered one thing from this knowledge card, that's the one.

sources.diff: Initial creation. 33-section structural extraction from a prior recorded conversation with Rodrigo, compressed by an AI assistant and re-supplied by Jake. Preserved the full 33-point scaffold verbatim under structural_observations. Added three interpretive layers on top: confirms_existing_bedrock (10 validations against indexed artifacts), new_facts_surfaced (10 net-new facts), gaps_and_flags (10 threads to pull). Included rodbot_implications section tying the founder's framing to specific shifts in posture, character, and red lines.
